data.title: Bank of America Bets on 2 Little-Known ‘Strong Buy’ AI Chip Stocks
data.description:
The surge in AI investment is rippling through the semiconductor supply chain in ways that go beyond GPUs and CPUs. Building and scaling modern data centers requires a growing amount of specialized connectivity and networking silicon, creating opportunities for companies positioned in the high-speed data movement segment of the market.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

While Nvidia grabs headlines for powering AI brains, a different class of chipmakers is quietly building the nervous system that connects them. As data centers scale to support ever-larger models, the ability to move information quickly and reliably between servers, racks, and processors is becoming just as important as raw computing power.

This shift is reshaping where value is created in the semiconductor ecosystem. Training and running modern AI workloads requires massive volumes of data to be transmitted at extremely high speeds, often across complex networks of hardware. That has turned connectivity, signal integrity, and power efficiency into strategic priorities for hyperscalers racing to expand their AI infrastructure.

The result is a growing focus on the less glamorous layers of the stack – the chips that manage, amplify, and route data rather than process it. These components may not perform calculations, but without them, AI systems cannot scale effectively. As bottlenecks move from compute to communication, spending is beginning to follow, opening the door for suppliers positioned at this critical junction of performance and efficiency.

Bank of America’s 5-star tech expert Vivek Arya has taken this logic to its natural endpoint – he has picked out two lesser-known AI chip stocks to buy at current levels. These are companies connected to the industry – in exactly the supporting roles that are now growing ever more important.

After using TipRanks’ database, we found out that each ticker has also received enough support from other Wall Street analysts to earn a “Strong Buy” consensus rating. Let’s take a closer look.

Credo Technology Group (CRDO)

We’ll start with Credo Technology Group, the California tech company that has both hands fully enmeshed in networking and connectivity. Credo provides solutions and products for the high-speed bandwidth and wired connections that make data infrastructure function – and without which, AI capability would not be possible.

Prominent among Credo’s products are AECs, PCIe retimers, ethernet retimers, and optical solutions. These products provide the direct, high-capacity connections that interlink server racks, connect accelerator clusters, and provide essential GPU-to-GPU connections. Credo assembles these systems based on a high-end core technology, a solid architecture, and a system-level approach that provides a firm foundation.

In practical terms, what this means is that Credo can support AI infrastructure for the largest hyperscalers. The company’s products can meet the high-speed reliability that makes interconnections at the largest scales possible – connecting AI clusters with more than 1 million GPUs while maintaining low latency and high bandwidth, and, at the same time, allowing AI operations to scale up and scale out.

Recent trends – the expansion of AI data centers, the growth of generative and agentic AI – have been good to Credo – and that’s putting it mildly. Even competition from such major names as Marvell and Astera hasn’t been able to derail Credo’s strength. The company reported a huge 272% year-over-year revenue gain in its last quarterly release, which covered fiscal 2Q26. The top line in the quarter reached $268 million and beat the forecast by $33 million. The company’s non-GAAP EPS figure, of 67 cents, was 17 cents per share better than had been anticipated.

Bank of America’s Arya, who is rated by TipRanks among the top 2% of his Wall Street peers, notes both Credo’s status as an early provider of AECs and other high-end data center networking tech, and its ability to withstand competition. He writes, “As a pioneer of the AEC technology, CRDO had enjoyed near 90% share through 2024. Starting in 2025, competition from MRVL/ALAB emerged, with CRDO market share likely declining to the ~70-75% range. Nevertheless, we view CRDO as the technological, reliability, tech transition, and customer engagement leader in AECs with first mover advantage. If CRDO can maintain 50% market share through CY30E, we view AEC sales alone could ~4x to $3.5bn from ~$900bn in CY25E, or $10+ EPS power at current 65% GM, excluding any new meaningful impact from new optical ALCs (active LED cables), PCIe retimers, or omniconnect gearboxes. This is also in-line with: (1) CRDO’s recent $10bn total company TAM outlook by CY30, of which believe CRDO can capture 40-50% overall market share at ~$4-5bn in annual sales, or (2) ~8-10% share assumption of total ~$60bn AI connectivity TAM (5% of $1.2Tn+ AI TAM).”

Quantifying his stance, Arya gives the shares a Buy rating with a $200 price target that implies a 56% upside potential for the coming year. (To watch Arya’s track record, click here)

Shares in CRDO are currently trading for $128.02, and the $224.44 average target price implies a one-year gain of 75%. Overall, the stock holds a Strong Buy consensus rating, based on 12 reviews with a lopsided split of 11 Buys to 1 Hold. (See CRDO stock forecast)

data.title: Goldman Sachs Doubles Down on These 2 Stocks
data.description:
With January nearly in the rearview mirror, Wall Street’s crystal-ball gazers are already sketching out what the rest of 2026 might look like. A few themes are coming into focus: the U.S. economy appears set to keep chugging along, with AI once again acting as a key engine of growth. The Federal Reserve has shifted into easing mode, although just how far rates will fall remains an open question.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The bigger question is whether growth can remain stronger than many expect. According to the team at Goldman Sachs Asset Management, the conditions are already in place for a solid year ahead.

“In the US, activity should stay robust, underpinned by strong AI-related capital expenditure, still-easy financial conditions, and a positive fiscal impulse from the ‘One Big Beautiful Bill,’” the strategists wrote. “Against this macro backdrop, monetary policy is likely to remain a key differentiator across economies… We expect the Fed to cut rates further in 2026, but above-trend growth argues for only limited additional easing. That said, the appointment of a dovish new Fed Chair could point to somewhat more room for cuts, depending on the extent to which the new Chair manages to convince the rest of the FOMC of their policy views.”

With that bullish outlook in mind, Goldman’s stock analysts have recently doubled down on two names. We opened up the TipRanks database to see what’s behind those calls – and what the firm is betting on.

Enphase Energy (ENPH)

We’ll start in the solar industry, where Enphase is a leader in the US – and world – market for residential solar power installations. The company is best known as a supplier of microinverter-based solar-plus-storage systems; microinverters are a vital piece of every solar installation, converting the natural DC output of the photovoltaic panels into AC electric current that can be used in the home.

In addition, Enphase also provides the necessary ancillaries to make a solar installation work for the customer: the connection solutions to link the solar power generation to storage batteries, and an app-based platform for intelligent energy management. The system is designed to bring a high-tech approach to the practical level of residential solar: from power generation to power storage to customer control.

In recent weeks, Enphase has made some announcements that investors should take notice of. In December, the company announced that it had expanded its safe harbor agreement with a solar financing company and expects to generate some $55 million in additional revenue from 4Q25 into 1Q26. And on January 12, the company announced the start of production shipments of its new IQ9 microinverters in the US. The new inverters are manufactured in the US and are Enphase’s first to use gallium nitride (GaN) technology.

All of this leads into Enphase’s financial results. We haven’t yet seen the 4Q25 report, but the 3Q25 results showed a two-year high in quarterly revenue. The $410.4 million top line was up 8% year-over-year and beat the forecast by almost $41 million. Enphase realized a non-GAAP earnings figure of 90 cents per share, 24 cents per share better than had been previously expected.

Laying out the Goldman Sachs view here, analyst Brian Lee explains why this solar company is in a good place to keep running forward. He writes, “We see ENPH poised to enter 2026 with some momentum, including lean channel inventories that provide a cleaner set up heading into 2026. At the same time, ENPH’s valuation remains at or near all-time lows, and risk-reward appears to be more favorable than it has been in some time, especially considering investor sentiment remains sharply skewed negative (based on elevated short interest levels) despite what we see as potential for a near-term beat vs. consensus expectations.”

Accordingly, Lee upgraded the rating on ENPH to a Buy (from a Neutral) and sets a $45 price target that implies an upside of 14% for the next 12 months. (To watch Lee’s track record, click here)

That view is decidedly more bullish than the consensus on Enphase, which gets a Hold from Wall Street – based on 22 reviews that include 7 Buys, 10 Holds, and 5 Sells. The shares are priced at $39.46, and their $39.05 average price target indicates they are currently fully valued. (See ENPH stock forecast)

data.title: Analysts Predict Up to 400% Spike for These 2 ‘Strong Buy’ Penny Stocks
data.description:
While mega-cap tech stocks with trillion-dollar valuations tend to dominate market headlines, they aren’t the only games in town. Smaller companies, especially those at the low end of the price spectrum, can offer meaningful upside. These are the so-called penny stocks – traditionally associated with shares trading for just pennies, but today defined more broadly as stocks priced below $5.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

At these low price levels, even small absolute price increases can translate into outsized percentage gains. Of course, the same dynamic applies to losses, making careful stock selection especially important.

This is where separating the penny stocks poised to take off from those likely to stay in the dumps becomes critical. One way to narrow the field is by looking at where Wall Street analysts are placing their bets – and biotech is high on that list. In this sector, stocks are often driven by clear catalysts such as clinical trial data, regulatory decisions, and new product launches. These events can quickly shift investor sentiment and materially affect valuations.

One of the more bullish voices on biotech’s revival is UBS analyst Michael Yee, who sees improving conditions across the sector.

“Following prolonged underperformance driven by pricing pressures, FDA uncertainty, capital scarcity, and high interest rates, biotech is exiting a cyclical trough. The macro environment and sector fundamentals are improving – capital access is reopening, FDA approvals are rising, clinical data momentum is building, and M&A is accelerating. We expect investor confidence to recover, positioning biotech for strong performance in 2026,” Yee opined.

With that backdrop in mind, we turned to the TipRanks database to zero in on two biotech penny stocks drawing strong support from Wall Street analysts. Both carry a Strong Buy consensus rating and significant upside potential, with one offering room for gains of up to 400%. Let’s take a closer look at what’s behind that optimism.

Acumen Pharmaceuticals (ABOS)

We’ll start with Acumen Pharmaceuticals, a micro-cap biotech company taking on one of medicine’s biggest challenges – Alzheimer’s disease. Acumen is developing new treatments aimed at slowing the progression of the illness, with a mission the company sums up as helping patients “hold on to their moments.” It’s a goal that puts the company in the middle of a massive and growing market opportunity. According to the Alzheimer’s Association, more than 7 million people in the U.S. are living with Alzheimer’s disease, a figure projected to climb to nearly 13 million by 2050 as the population ages.

Unlike many approaches that have focused on amyloid plaques, Acumen is taking a somewhat unconventional path by targeting toxic amyloid-beta oligomers (AβOs) – small, soluble forms of amyloid that research suggests may act as early triggers and long-term drivers of synaptic dysfunction and neurodegeneration in Alzheimer’s.

Acumen’s lead drug candidate, sabirnetug, is a humanized monoclonal antibody engineered specifically to target these toxic soluble AβOs, with the goal of preserving neurological function by keeping these toxins from binding to synapses and wreaking havoc.

In its Phase 1 clinical trial (INTERCEPT-AD), sabirnetug showed a favorable safety profile with low overall rates of ARIA-E – a type of brain swelling sometimes seen with anti-amyloid therapies – along with evidence of target engagement and positive biomarker effects, including reductions in amyloid plaque measures at the higher doses tested. The therapy’s potential was further underscored by the Fast Track designation granted by the FDA for the treatment of early Alzheimer’s disease, a regulatory status that helps streamline the development and review of promising medicines.

With those early results in hand, this past November, Acumen announced that it had begun dosing the first participant in the Phase 2 ALTITUDE-AD trial’s open-label extension, extending treatment for an additional year in participants who completed the initial controlled portion of the study. That Phase 2 trial evaluates sabirnetug’s ability to slow cognitive and functional decline in people with early Alzheimer’s, and the company currently plans to share topline results in late 2026.

While sabirnetug continues to move through clinical testing, Acumen is also working on a next-generation strategy aimed at improving how antibodies reach the brain. In July 2025, the company entered a strategic collaboration with JCR Pharmaceuticals to develop an amyloid-beta oligomer-targeted Enhanced Brain Delivery (EBD) therapy. Through this partnership, Acumen is pairing its AβO-selective antibodies with JCR’s blood-brain-barrier-penetrating technology, known as J-Brain Cargo, to create drug candidates designed for far greater central nervous system exposure. Acumen expects non-clinical data to support the EBD program in early 2026, at which point it will have the exclusive option to select and advance up to two development candidates emerging from the collaboration.

With a major catalyst ahead and ABOS shares trading at $1.98, Stifel’s 5-star analyst Paul Matteis thinks now is the right time to make a move.

“With the upcoming ph2 ALTITUDE-AD readout for sabirnetug in early Alzheimer’s expected in late 2026, ABOS has an important year ahead of them. After the stock has been largely tied to the progression of the donanemab/lecanameb launches – with shares trading at/below cash – 2026 represents a major potential inflection point for the stock… We’re encouraged by the initial data from sabirnetug’s ph1b, which illustrate that the oligomer hypothesis has the potential to clinically differentiate vs older-generation abetas. To that point, the fact that the ph2 completed enrollment on time reflects there’s continued demand from patients for novel treatments. We like the design/set-up of ALTITUDE-AD, offering a real evaluation of the oligomer mechanism across validated AD clinical scales,” Matteis noted.

To this end, Matteis rates ABOS a Buy, and his $10 price target points to a massive 405% upside over the next year. (To watch Matteis’ track record, click here)

Overall, the Street agrees that the outlook is bullish. The stock has 5 recent Buy ratings, and no Holds or Sells, forming a unanimous Strong Buy consensus. The $6.40 average target price implies a 223% gain on a one-year basis. (See ABOS stock forecast)

data.title: These 2 ‘Perfect 10’ Stocks Are Winning Wall Street’s Confidence
data.description:
Successful investing rarely comes down to nailing every trade. More often, it’s about building a portfolio that consistently leans toward the right names over time. Getting that mix right is where most of the real work happens.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

That’s where TipRanks’ Smart Score comes in. The AI-driven tool is designed to cut through the noise by pulling together a wide range of market signals – from analyst activity and insider transactions to financial performance and broader investor sentiment – and organizing them into a single, usable framework.

Each publicly traded stock is then assigned a score on a scale of 1 to 10, with a “Perfect 10” representing the strongest overall setup. The rating reflects how a stock stacks up against a set of factors that have historically aligned with outperformance. It’s not a crystal ball, but it does offer a data-backed snapshot of how the odds may be stacking up.

With that in mind, we took a look through the TipRanks database to identify stocks that currently carry a Perfect 10 Smart Score – and that have recently picked up support from the Street’s analysts. Below are two that stand out.

Pony AI (PONY)

We’ll start in the world of AI and autonomous vehicles, where Pony AI, our first ‘Perfect 10’ stock, is operating in the Chinese market. The company keeps two headquarters offices, one in Fremont, California and the other in Guangzhou, China. Since starting in 2016, Pony has become a leader in China’s autonomous vehicle sector, developing self-driving vehicles and robotaxis.

The company’s technology is based on perception – high end sensors that tell the AI where the vehicle is and what is surrounding it; prediction – that allows the AI to extrapolate the movements of pedestrians and vehicles in the area; planning & control – that allows the AI system to plot a course and follow it; and infrastructure and hardware, including everything from the on-board software and control systems to the remote servers that provide computing support for the system. Pony has integrated more than 20 redundant safety features into its autonomous vehicles, understanding that this tech will never expand if the public cannot trust it.

Pony’s robotaxi, PonyPilot, is available in Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou, and Shenzhen, and customers can access the service via a mobile app. The company has been using its sixth-generation automated robotaxi vehicles for several years, and this past November launched its Gen-7 robotaxis. Pony is on track to put 3,000 robotaxis in operation by the end of this year. In addition, Pony has an autonomous robotruck service in operation, on several routes in eastern China.

Pony has been seeing strong success in commercializing its robotaxi operations. Revenue from robotaxis was up 89.5% year-over-year in 3Q25, the last period reported, and total quarterly revenues hit US$25.4 million – for a 72% y/y gain. Like many tech firms, the company typically runs a net operating loss; in Q3, this came to 14 cents per share, in non-GAAP measures.

For Citi analyst Jeff Chung, there are multiple reasons for investors to take an upbeat look here, especially over the near-term. Listing those, he writes, “We open 90-day upside catalyst watch on Pony AI, considering 6 events ahead: (1) 4Q25 results likely to be decent with one-off gain (estimated ~100x return on Moore Threads investment), (2) Waymo’s potential 1Q26 financing round at an implied valuation of 280x 2025 P/S which may re-rate Pony AI’s valuation (87x 2025 P/S), (3) Improving investor sentiment on Pony’s potential entry into Southbound stock connect in Jun’26, (4) USChina competition in tech area potentially encouraging China to launch a more aggressive ADAS/Robotaxi policy, (5) New-gen Robotaxi further cutting ADK cost by 20% around Apr-2026, (6) Asset-light business developing well.”

Putting this into quantifiable terms, Chung rates PONY as a Buy, with a $24.50 price target that implies a one-year upside of 48%. (To watch Chung’s track record, click here)

Overall, Pony holds a Strong Buy consensus rating from the Street, based on 4 reviews that split 3 to 1 favoring Buy over Hold. The shares are priced at $16.58, and the $24.95 average price target indicates a 50.5% gain in store for the next 12 months. (See PONY stock forecast)

data.title: ‘Signs of Resilience’: J.P. Morgan Says the Macro Is Holding Up – and Likes These 2 Stocks
data.description:
With more than halfway through January already behind us, investors are taking stock of a year that repeatedly challenged consensus thinking. Throughout 2025, markets were forced to navigate an unusually noisy macro backdrop, where policy headlines, trade frictions, and shifting growth expectations kept volatility elevated and confidence in check.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Inflation concerns lingered, economists trimmed forecasts, and uncertainty around government policy made positioning tricky. Yet, despite those pressures, economic activity proved more durable than expected, and risk assets ultimately found their footing as the year progressed.

That underlying resilience sits at the heart of how JPMorgan’s global investment strategy team is framing the outlook for 2026.

“2025 gave investors plenty of reasons to doubt the outlook. Tariffs, immigration and policy noise fueled volatility and forced many economists to trim growth forecasts. Yet the economy kept moving – in Q3 2025, real gross domestic product (GDP) grew at a 4.3% annualized rate, far exceeding the initial expectation of roughly 3.3%. Our view is that this resilience extends into 2026,” the strategists opined.

“The reason we’re comfortable leaning that way is that the key macro constraint is loosening, and it starts with inflation and the Federal Reserve (Fed). We anticipate inflation to remain rangebound, which should allow the Fed to maintain an easing bias. In fact, we’re anticipating one additional rate cut this year – not a signal of aggressive easing, but a move that keeps rates on a lower trajectory,” they added.

Building on that view, JPM’s stock analysts are singing the praises of two stocks they see as well positioned to benefit if the macro backdrop continues to hold up.

Both names have earned upgraded ratings from JPM’s analysts – but does the rest of the Street share that confidence? We ran the two through the TipRanks database to find out.

Southwest Airlines (LUV)

The first stock on our radar here is Southwest Airlines, the leading low-cost carrier in the US air travel market. Southwest is based in Texas, at Love Field near Dallas, and has been in operation since 1971.

Southwest offers quality services at low prices, and cuts out the frills that other airlines use to bring in customers. In addition, Southwest practices smart fleet planning, and operates only Boeing 733-model aircraft. This ensures that all of the company’s flight and cabin crews can fly any aircraft in the company inventory, and drastically cuts down on training times. On the maintenance side, sticking to one family of aircraft allows ground crews to train more efficiently and makes parts procurement easier; all of this contributes to an overall streamlined operation that is capable of absorbing systemic shocks.

We’ll see Southwest’s 4Q25 results on January 29, but for now we can look back at Q3. In that period, the company reported a top line of $6.9 billion. This was up a modest 1.2% from the prior year, but it beat the forecast by $20 million. Southwest’s non-GAAP EPS of 11 cents was based on a net income of $58 million; the EPS was 15 cents per share better than had been expected.

JPM’s Jamie Baker sees potential for share price gains here. Explaining why, he says of Southwest, “We believe the potential for a $5 EPS guide from Southwest to be attractively probable. Such a guide would handily dwarf the $2.98 consensus for 2026, as well as all prevailing individual forecasts. Granted, the market may not immediately embrace a guide of this magnitude, owing to Southwest’s somewhat checkered guidance history, which we believe lies at the root of its decision to embrace the more streamlined EPS format. However, even a sub-$5 sellside/buyside embrace coupled with static valuation frameworks could prove sufficient in lifting shares above the $50 mark in the short run, clearing the way towards a potential $60 future, in our view.”

Baker recently made a double upgrade here, moving LUV from Underweight to Overweight (i.e., Buy). This is accompanied by a $60 price target that implies a 40% share price increase in the coming year. (To watch Baker’s track record, click here)

On balance, most on the Street are more cautious here. The 15 recent analyst reviews break down to 4 to Buy, 9 to Hold, and 2 to Sell, for a Hold consensus rating. LUV is trading for $42.85 and its $43.20 average target suggests that the stock will stay rangebound for the time being.  (See LUV stock forecast)

data.title: Goldman Sachs Sees a Tough Backdrop for IT Stocks, but Says You Can Still Win with These 3 ‘Strong Buy’ Names
data.description:
Talk about tech these days, and most people will flash directly to AI. But Goldman Sachs analyst Katherine Murphy is digging deeper, uncovering opportunities across IT hardware. The premise is straightforward: hardware still matters, at every layer of the stack.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Like any industry, however, IT hardware is contending with real challenges. The sector is still digesting the aftereffects of the 2023 demand trough, when post-pandemic needs were largely pulled forward. Since then, higher tariffs and inflation-driven increases in input costs have weighed on performance. Adding to the pressure, expectations for PC demand this year have softened.

At the same time, where AI investment is taking place, it is beginning to provide tangible support. Rather than lifting the entire sector, that spending is flowing into specific areas of IT hardware where replacement cycles are still unfolding, creating selective opportunities even as investors continue to debate the durability of AI demand, the timing of broader refresh cycles, and the impact of higher costs on margins.

That selective dynamic underpins Murphy’s outlook: “We are selectively positive on stocks that we view as best exposed to categories that still are in the expansion phase of their multi-year refresh cycle, specifically x86 servers, enterprise storage and campus networking… We prefer IT hardware vendors and distributors where we see upside to consensus estimates & which we believe screen attractively against three key investor debates into 2026: (1) How sustainable is AI demand?; (2) Where are we in the refresh cycle for PCs, servers, storage, and campus networking?; and (3) How will higher input costs impact margins and demand?”

Against this backdrop, Murphy pinpointed three IT stocks that could still deliver gains even in a challenging environment. And if you don’t want to take her word for it, TipRanks data shows that all three carry Strong Buy consensus ratings. Let’s dive in.

Dell Technologies (DELL)

The first Goldman pick we’re looking at, Dell, is a well-known name from the PC world. The ~$80 billion company got its start back in 1994; in its modern incarnation, it is the result of the merger between Dell and EMC in 2016. Dell is an important supplier of PCs and laptops, as well as peripheral hardware – monitors, headsets, gaming accessories. In addition, the company also produces and distributes vital AI-capable hardware such as servers and memory storage.

On the AI side, Dell offers a wide portfolio of AI infrastructure products. As noted, these can include servers and memory storage; the company also produces hardware for AI networking, data management, and cyber resilience. The next generation of AI is going to be smaller and faster, and Dell is already producing AI-capable workstations, PCs, and laptops.

In an important – and related – announcement, Dell made public on January 6 this year the release of its new XPS laptops. This is a reestablishment of one of the company’s iconic products, and the new XPS 14 and XPS 16 are already available. The gaming version features Intel’s Core Ultra 200HX processors, while the flagship model has AMD’s 2nd Gen Ryze 9000X3D chip. The new XPS series is AI-capable, and the XPS-13 is due out later this year. Dell has also recently released new monitors, including the first 52-inch 6K monitor to hit the markets, and a 32-inch 4K QD-OLED monitor.

At the end of this past November, Dell released its results for fiscal 3Q26 – and in that quarter the company brought in $27 billion in revenue, a third quarter record that was up 11% year-over-year – but missed the forecast by $296 million. At the bottom line, Dell’s non-GAAP diluted EPS grew 17% year-over-year to reach $2.59, beating the forecast by 11 cents per share.

Katherine Murphy, in her write-up for Goldman, says of Dell: “We believe that DELL will continue to be a leader in the AI server OEM market for neocloud customers, supported by the company’s differentiated engineering expertise & services capabilities. We see upside to DELL’s outlook for AI server revenue to grow at a 20-25% CAGR (F2026-30) at mid single digit% EBIT margins from (1) upward capex revisions from tier two cloud services providers; and (2) more material demand from sovereign & enterprise customers for AI infrastructure, which should be margin accretive.”

Looking ahead, Murphy explains why she believes the stock should post gains this year, writing, “While outlook for growth in traditional servers and storage is more modest (mid single digit % growth in core ISG) and PCs demand should be challenged by higher pricing, we are encouraged by DELL’s self-help actions to gain profitable share, which should serve to partially offset any consolidated margin pressure from greater AI server mix or increased commodity costs.”

Murphy follows these comments with a Buy rating and a 12-month price target of $165, implying an upside of 48.5% by early 2027.

Overall, Dell’s Strong Buy consensus rating is based on 16 analyst reviews that break down to 13 Buys, 2 Holds, and 1 Sell. The stock’s $111.07 trading price and $164 average target price together indicate a 48% gain in store for the year ahead. (See DELL stock forecast)

data.title: Piper Sandler Puts its Weight Behind 2 ‘Strong Buy’ Cancer Stocks
data.description:
Cancer remains one of the world’s deadliest challenges. Second only to heart disease, it claims one in every six lives globally – about 10 million deaths each year – and the numbers are still climbing as lifestyle factors such as smoking, poor diet, and inactivity continue to take their toll.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

For the biopharmaceutical industry, that grim reality has translated into an enormous opportunity. According to Precedence Research, the global market for cancer therapies was valued at $86.2 billion in 2024 and rose to $92.5 billion last year. Looking ahead, it is projected to nearly double to $175.2 billion by 2034, fueled by breakthroughs in novel treatment approaches and a growing push toward personalized medicine.

Against this backdrop, Piper Sandler’s 5-star analyst Kelsey Goodwin has picked two cancer-focused biopharma stocks she believes are especially well positioned to benefit from these trends.

Using the TipRanks platform, we took a closer look at her picks – and how they stack up against the broader Wall Street consensus. Let’s dive in.

Olema Pharmaceuticals (OLMA)

Kicking off Piper Sandler’s list is Olema, a clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company focused squarely on women’s cancers. The company’s efforts center on a single, potentially best-in-class breast cancer therapy, palazestrant, which anchors its entire R&D pipeline.

Olema’s pipeline currently has 7 tracks based on palazestrant. The research tracks range from Phase 1 to Phase 3, and are testing palazestrant as a monotherapy and as a combo therapy with various other cancer drugs. Several of the clinical studies are being conducted in conjunction with larger partner companies, Novartis and Pfizer.

Palazestrant, also called pala, is a novel compound, designed to be orally dosed. The drug is an ER (estrogen receptor) antagonist, and blocks cancer cell growth and proliferation signals that are ER-mediated. The drug is under investigation as a treatment for ER+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer.

We should note that OLMA stock jumped sharply in November of last year. That jump in value occurred after Roche, a competitor company, announced positive clinical trial results from its own candidate, giredestrant. This is a new drug in the same class as pala, and its success is considered a positive indicator for the future success of palazestrant.

In her comments on Olema, and its leading candidate, Piper Sandler’s Goodwin writes: “OLMA is currently advancing a potential best-in-class CERAN/SERD, palazestrant, capable of complete ER antagonism, potent ER degradation, and holds the promise of becoming the next backbone endocrine therapy in the large ER+/HER2- breast cancer market. Following years of the Street equating all SERD/SERM/CERANs, the lead CERANs are pulling ahead on positive competitor data that surprised to the upside in 4Q25 and has begun to revive the bull case for lead oral SERDs. data.title: RGTI and QUBT: This Analyst Sees the Next Jump in Quantum Stocks
data.description:
Quantum computing is the latest shiny thing in tech, a new technology operating at the leading edge of the computer industry. It leverages the natural superposition properties of subatomic physics to enable faster rates of calculation; instead of the 0–1 states of binary systems, quantum computers can operate in both positions simultaneously, allowing for faster computing times and higher-density information storage.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The demand for that kind of computing power is only accelerating. From biopharma drug discovery and logistics optimization to advanced financial modeling, entire industries are running up against the limits of today’s hardware. Quantum computing, especially when delivered through the cloud, could offer a way through that bottleneck. Major players such as Amazon, IBM, and Microsoft are already rolling out quantum cloud platforms, and those cost-efficient, scalable deployments are widely expected to shape how the market develops.

Putting some numbers on the sector, the Research and Markets outlet predicts that the global quantum computing market will expand from last year’s $3.52 billion to $20.2 billion in 2030. This translates to an impressive 41.8% CAGR over the forecast period.

That kind of expansion tends to attract attention, and Rosenblatt analyst John McPeake is already zeroing in on the sector’s standouts. He sees compelling upside in Rigetti Computing (NASDAQ:RGTI) and Quantum Computing (NASDAQ:QUBT), projecting gains of more than 50% over the next 12 months. Let’s take a closer look.

Rigetti Computing

First on our Rosenblatt-backed quantum list is Rigetti Computing, a company that sits adjacent to Silicon Valley in its Berkeley headquarters. Over the past decade-plus, Rigetti has carved out a niche for itself designing, building, and marketing superconducting quantum integrated circuits and processor chips, and the supercooled dilution systems needed for those circuits and chips to function in a quantum state. The supercooling systems, while advanced and functional, highlight one of the headwinds facing the quantum computing industry as a whole: the required cryogenic refrigeration units are large and expensive, and add substantially to the cost of operating quantum computers.

When it comes to quantum computers, Rigetti has several designs and models available commercially. The company’s flagship Ankaa-3 system uses a non-modular chip, while the Cepheus-1-36Q operates through four modular quantum computing processor units. Rigetti’s smaller Novera QPUs are also modular, and capable of being linked in tandem – this feature allows customers to put together a boosted quantum computer, with more power than a single unit.

In a note that should interest investors, Rigetti announced earlier this month a revised date for ‘general availability’ of the upcoming Cepheus-1-108Q. The new system makes use of the company’s modular quantum chip architecture based on twelve 9-qubit chiplets. General availability is expected near the end of 1Q26.

In Rigetti’s last reported financial quarter, 3Q25, the company’s revenue of $1.9 million was down almost 19% year-over-year and came in $223K below the forecast. Rigetti operates a net loss, like many cutting-edge tech innovators; the company’s non-GAAP EPS was ($0.03) – although we should note that this was 1 cent per share better than had been expected.

So, the revenue generated here is still rather small. However, for analyst McPeake, there should be plenty of growth heading Rigetti’s way. The key point here is the quality of Rigetti’s quantum computers and their fit to a wide range of real-world applications.

“We like Rigetti’s modular approach to qubit scaling and their internal fab approach. Although error rates and timelines are a bit of an issue right now, we are giving them the benefit of the doubt relative to the postponed Cepheus 99.5% two qubit gate fidelity target in 1Q,” McPeake noted.

“Street forecasts generally do not extend beyond 2030–but we think the bulk of the value add in this market is likely to accrue after quantum advantage is achieved in the 2030 timeframe for real world problems in materials science, chemistry, and pharmaceuticals. The company segments their revenue into professional services, hardware, and quantum as a service. We are forecasting a top line CAGR of 83% through 2030, and then 56% from there until 2035, when the company’s revenues will be 54% quantum hardware sales, 41% QaaS, and 5% pro-serve, vs. the 2025 figure of just 12% hardware, 4% QaaS, and 84% professional services,” the analyst added.

McPeake goes on to rate RGTI shares as a Buy, with a $40 price target that suggests a one-year upside potential of 56%. (To watch McPeake’s track record, click here)

Overall, Rigetti’s Moderate Buy consensus rating is based on 11 recent reviews with a split of 8 Buys to 3 Holds. The stock is priced at $25.62 and its $39.78 average target price nearly matches Rosenblatt’s. (See RGTI stock forecast)

Consumer spending played a big role, rising 3.5%, while exports also chipped in. “Considering the resilience that the market showed last year,” Stoltzfus says, “and the capability it showed to rally on economic, corporate, and consumer fundamentals it looks to us like the future as uncertain as it always is persists in having opportunities for investors with right-sized expectations of how various asset classes and the businesses tied to them navigate change. The Oppenheimer stock analysts are following Stoltzfus’ path, and looking for stocks that can gain as resilience drives the markets. They’ve picked two, and we’ve used the TipRanks platform to see how the Oppenheimer view compares to the Street’s consensus.

Amprius Technologies (AMPX)

The first Oppenheimer pick we’ll look at is Amprius Technologies, a company that is positioning itself to take a leading role as the economy shifts more toward the use of electric power. Amprius specializes in the design and manufacture of high-energy-density, high-capacity lithium-ion batteries that make use of silicon anodes. Energy density is a key metric in battery technology, as it measures how much power is available to the device using the storage cell.

Amprius boasts that it can deliver batteries with the highest energy density on the market, along with a long life cycle and ultra-fast charging times. The company backs this up with safety and reliability – making sure that its batteries have passed rigorous checks that include the U.S. military’s nail penetration test. We’ve all heard the horror stories of lithium battery fires – Amprius makes sure that its batteries are as safe as possible.

The company’s batteries offer 100% longer running times, a -30 to +60 degree centigrade operating temperature range, more than 1,300 lifetime charge cycles, and a mere 6-minute charging time to 80% capacity. The batteries are designed for a wide range of applications, including manned and unmanned aircraft, light electric vehicles such as e-bikes, and AI and robotics. Amprius maintains its headquarters in Fremont, California, and has four manufacturing facilities in East Asia.

In addition to its role in an increasingly important sector, Amprius is in the midst of a leadership transition. As of January 1, new CEO Tom Stepien has taken the helm. Stepien had been President of the company since May of last year. Former CEO Dr. Kang Sun will remain affiliated with Amprius, and will be present as an Executive Advisor.

In its 3Q25 report, Amprius declared a top line of $21.4 million, a record for the company. The Q3 revenue beat the forecast by $4.6 million, and was up a whopping 172% year-over-year. At the bottom line, Amprius ran a net loss of 3 cents per share – but even this was 3 cents per share better than had been expected. We should note that Amprius’ stock is up 187.5% in the last 12 months.

Top analyst Colin Rusch, who is rated among the top 1% of Wall Street’s analysts, notes the new CEO and the strong manufacturing and goes on from there. He writes of Amprius, “As AMPX makes a CEO transition with President Tom Stepien taking over at CEO, we believe the company is entering a new phase of its development. With new financial leadership in place as well as 2GWH+ of manufacturing capacity available, including several new manufacturers and associated supply chain partners meeting the requirements of the US National Defense Authorization Act (“NDAA”), we expect the company to focus on a long-term pricing strategy intended to maximize value capture as it transitions customers from sampling to production orders… We continue to believe AMPX has a multi-year lead on high-density, lightweight battery technology for aerospace/defense applications and is poised to announce significant new customers.”

data.description:
IPOs tend to draw close attention because they offer a useful snapshot of overall market confidence. When companies are willing to go public, it signals that conditions are supportive enough to raise capital in the equity markets – and that investors are receptive to new listings.

In the years following the pandemic, that window was largely shut. Activity remained muted as volatility, inflation, and rising interest rates weighed on sentiment. That began to change last year, as the U.S. IPO market showed clear signs of revival. By mid-December, 342 companies had gone public in the U.S., representing a 57% increase year over year. Just as importantly, those offerings raised meaningfully more capital, with aggregate proceeds exceeding $75 billion – roughly 80% higher than the prior year.

Cooling inflation and the Federal Reserve’s shift toward interest-rate easing have helped improve equity market conditions, making stocks comparatively more attractive. At the same time, the U.S. market has continued to outperform many international peers, with the prospect of higher valuations and deeper liquidity helping to draw additional capital.

With that backdrop in mind, we can take a look at two recent IPOs that have caught Morgan Stanley’s attention. While both companies are on the firm’s radar, its views on them differ in important ways. We also check the TipRanks database to see how the broader analyst community stacks up on each name. Let’s dive in.

Medline (MDLN)

The first new IPO stock on our list is not a new company at all. Illinois-based Medline, founded in 1966, is a major player in the healthcare industry, and is well-known as a provider of medical-surgical products, as well as supply chain solutions for all ‘points of care’ in the field. Medline has a broad portfolio that includes such items as gloves and medical apparel; medical and diagnostic equipment; lab supplies; and nutrition, skin care, and pharmacy supplies – and that’s just a short sampling of the list.

In all, Medline offers more than 335,000 products, under a wide range of brand names. On the logistical side, the company employs over 43,000 people, operates more than 29 million square feet of warehousing, and can provide next-day shipping for 95% of its US customer base.

Medline’s stock hit the market on Wednesday, December 17, last year, and the IPO closed the next day. The event was successful – and in fact, was the largest IPO of 2025. Medline sold a total of 216,034,482 shares at $29 each, and raised gross proceeds of $6.26 billion. The underwriters also exercised their option, and purchased an additional 32,405,172 shares of MDLN. The company has a market cap of ~$34 billion.

Patrick Wood covers this stock for Morgan Stanley. He sees the company as a solid option for investors, based in part on its position as the largest medical supply distributor in the US market. He writes, “Given the unique business model as well as the large size and scale of the business, there are no perfect comps for Medline. However, we think positioning in-line with diversified and integrated medtech/tools names is warranted given Medline’s vertical integration and large scale presence within the healthcare eco-system (similar to TMO’s integration with US HC), despite near-term tariff pressures. Additionally… we think that the higher revenue growth-rates and more defensive underlying end markets should support Medline’s positioning longer-term… With some of the most sustainable and compounding growth (c. +8% with upside risk) in our sector, and a lower than average risk profile (no one-off clinical data, limited macro risk etc), we think the stock looks attractive…”

Wood follows up with an Overweight (Buy) rating here, and a $48 price target that implies a one-year upside potential of 10%. (To watch Wood’s track record, click here)

Since then, AI stocks have been driving the market’s bull run, and fueling gains in the tech sector – gains that, in some cases, have been measured in the trillions of dollars.

Wedbush’s Daniel Ives has been looking into the sector, and putting his finger on the pulse of leading stocks at all scales. The analyst describes AI as a new Industrial Revolution, with potential to reshape the economy and the larger world for decades to come.

Describing what’s likely to happen in 2026, Ives writes, “Heading into 2026 investors are both excited and nervous about the AI Revolution. On one hand this is a 4th Industrial Revolution taking shape with the US leading China on tech for the first in 30 years…while on the other hand the trillions of dollars needed to reach the heights of enterprise/consumer AI Revolution have caused nervousness among investors. Let’s take a closer look at them, and find out how Ives’ stance compares to the overall Wall Street view.

Palantir Technologies

The first of Ives’ picks that we’ll look at is Palantir, one of the better-known names in the AI industry. The company was founded in 2003, and offers its users a set of platforms and tools designed to make AI intuitive at the user end, by matching up the speed of AI computing power with the flexibility of human operators. Following this approach, Palantir has become a powerful mega-cap player in the field, with a ~$427 billion market cap valuation and a reputation as a leader in AI-powered data analytic services that are both innovative and high-end.

Palantir has achieved this by integrating AI tech directly into the user interface of its platform, a key feature that allows human system users to interact directly with the AI, entering data and queries in natural language and receiving replies the same way. This cuts out the need for complex computer or coding languages, and makes Palantir’s products accessible with a bare minimum of training.

In short, what Palantir does best is to make AI-powered data analysis readily available. The company’s products have proven widely popular and applicable at many levels, across the public and private sectors.

A couple of the company’s recent announcements show the range of its versatility: this month, Palantir announced a joint effort with Teton Ridge to bring real-time AI and computer vision to the rodeo niche, in a move that will enhance the sport’s experience for athletes, partners, and fans; and Palantir has announced a partnership with the US Navy, a $448 million plan to bring Palantir’s AI Platform into the Navy’s shipbuilding sector.

Palantir’s continuing fast growth was visible in its last earnings reports, which covered 3Q25. The company reported 63% year-over-year growth at the top line, which reached $1.18 billion and exceeded expectations by $89.5 million. The company had a bottom line, non-GAAP EPS of 21 cents, a figure that was 4 cents per share ahead of the forecasts, and it finished Q3 with deep pockets, $6.4 billion in cash and other liquid assets.

Palantir’s fast growth in recent years—the stock is up more than 2,400% in the last three years, and 171% over the past 12 months—has caused investors to worry about overvaluation, but Daniel Ives takes a bullish stance. For Ives, the key point here is the strong demand for the services that Palantir has to offer. He writes of the company, “PLTR continues to see unprecedented demand for AIP across both commercial and government landscapes by taking AI solutions that solve meaningful problems across organizations at enterprise scale driving new customer conversions and existing deal expansions. With the company making strategic moves to remain at the forefront of AI, we believe that PLTR has a golden path to become a trillion-dollar market cap company and will grow into its valuation as Karp and Co. remain one of the largest players in the AI Revolution.”

Ives quantifies his stance with an Outperform (Buy) rating, and a $230 price target that suggests room for a 28.5% gain for the shares over the next 12 months. (To watch Ives’ track record, click here)

data.title: S&P 500 Gains May Slow in 2026 — but Raymond James Says These 2 Stocks Could Beat the Market
data.description:
After posting a 16% gain last year, the S&P 500 capped off its third straight year of double-digit returns. But can 2026 deliver another solid year for equities, or is the market due for a cooldown?

That’s the debate taking shape on Wall Street – and according to Raymond James Chief Investment Officer Larry Adam, the pace of gains may begin to slow.

“Valuations for the S&P 500 have climbed to the 95th percentile, meaning price-to-earnings multiple expansion has little more to give,” Adam opined. “Returns will need to rely squarely on earnings growth. The good news? We expect earnings to rise about 12%, supporting our year-end S&P 500 target of 7,250. That means the return profile shifts from double-digit gains to mid-single digits. That said, conditions remain favorable: solid economic growth, an easing Fed, stable long-term rates, and healthy buybacks and dividends. Compared to recent years, however, you’ll want to keep expectations in check for equity returns.”

For investors aiming to go beyond that baseline and hunt for outsized upside, the Raymond James analysts have pinpointed two stocks they believe could meaningfully outperform the market in 2026. Both are rated as Strong Buys by the firm, and to put those convictions in context, we turned to the TipRanks database to see how the broader Street stacks up behind the calls. Let’s dive in.

Darling Ingredients (DAR)

Up first on our radar is Darling Ingredients, a company that has multiple lines of business, including food tech, agribusiness, and waste recycling. Darling’s core business is collecting the waste products of the animal husbandry, agriculture, and food industries and recycling them for use in a wide range of areas: as feedstock and food products, including animal feeds and human food additives, and as biofuels.

Darling has split its business into three branches: feed, food, and fuel. The first of these includes fats and proteins that are essential nutritious animal feeds and are used in both livestock and pet foods. Darling also uses its feedstock fats in its biofuel program. The company boasts that its rendering business is the largest in the world.

On the food side, Darling is a world leader in making and distributing collagens and gelatins. These products are produced in an international network of 16 factories. The gelatins have a vital role in the food industry as stabilizers, clarifiers, and thickeners, while the collagens are used in the health and wellness sectors.

Last month, Darling signed a definitive agreement with Belgium-based Tessenderlo, an industrial company with a strong footprint in the same feed and feedstock fields where Darling works. The two companies will combine their collagen and gelatin segments into a new firm to be called Nextida. Darling will hold an 85% stake in the new venture, which will not require a cash investment from either parent.

The last segment of Darling’s business, fuel, is home to the company’s biodiesel, biogas, and aviation fuels. These fuels are mainly produced by recycling cooking oil and animal fats, with the biogas being generated from food waste and pig manure. Darling’s fuel business is designed to produce a vital product from otherwise dangerous biological waste products.

On the financial side, Darling last reported results for 3Q25. In that quarter, the company’s top line came to $1.6 billion – a figure that beat the forecast by almost $62 million and was up 14% year-over-year. The company’s bottom line, the 12-cent GAAP EPS, was 11 cents per share lower than expected. We should note that at the end of 3Q25, Darling had $91.5 million in available liquid assets, compared to $75.9 million at the end of December 2024.

This stock has caught the eye of Raymond James’s Justin Jenkins, an analyst ranked among the top 1% on Wall Street, who lays out an upbeat take. The 5-star analyst writes, “RD and related sustainable fuels represent a structural growth element for DAR and industry peers – with pivotal regulatory catalysts (i.e., RVO finalization, SREs, Producer tax credits/45z) in the pipeline that can further solidify our positive outlook. This is all while DAR’s core ‘upstream’ operations remain a steady cash flow generator, and the stock price is nearing a point where the DGD JV stake is a call option value. All told, we think the business is at/near a trough for fundamentals and sentiment, and expect a solid recovery in earnings and the trading multiple… especially into 2026+.”

Jenkins gives DAR a Strong Buy rating, with a $60 price target that implies a 53.5% gain in the coming months. (To watch Jenkins’ track record, click here)

data.description:
It has been just over three years since OpenAI launched ChatGPT, setting off a rapid acceleration in generative AI development. The technology is often compared to past inflection points such as the Industrial Revolution or the rise of cloud computing – not because the parallels are perfect, but because each reshaped how work is done and where economic value is created.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Generative AI is now pushing directly into the information economy. It is automating many of the behind-the-scenes tasks that underpin digital work, spanning software development, finance, design, and customer service, and in the process reducing the amount of human labor required in these areas.

While disruptions among high-skill digital workers are already emerging and may intensify, the productivity gains are equally hard to ignore, as companies find they can operate faster and at greater scale with leaner teams. Regardless of one’s view on these shifts, their economic and social consequences are no longer hypothetical.

Analyst Arvind Ramnani, from Truist, has been watching this situation develop, and ably sums up where we’re going: “With GenAI becoming the new general purpose tech layer of the economy, we expect its adoption to compress the market impact of the last 40-years of enterprise tech into the next 5-10 years… As frontier-model platforms scale and enterprises incorporate copilots and agents into their workflows, it will likely result in lower payroll intensity per unit of output, higher productivity of human capital, and new software-like business models built on usage-based AI services rather than traditional licenses.”

“We believe that the economic rent pool will shift toward model providers, infra vendors, and application companies that can convert raw capabilities into domain specific systems, giving the GenAI ecosystem an outsized claim on future profit growth,” the analyst added.

Ramnani goes on to pick two AI stocks – each an AI-powered digital platform – that he believes will rise higher as AI adoption expands. We’ve pulled the latest data from TipRanks to get a feel for where these stocks stand on Wall Street; here’s a closer look at them.

Duolingo (DUOL)

The first Truist pick on our radar is Duolingo, the popular language-learning app. Most of us are familiar with Duolingo – the app has found a wide audience for its language education products, and for good reason. The digital world is growing more and more interconnected, and having a basic knowledge of languages beyond one’s own mother tongue provides an increasingly important advantage. Duolingo has tapped into that, and offers courses designed for an audience that wants to learn the basics of another language but does not necessarily have the time or resources for a formal language course.

Duolingo’s courses are offered online and lessons can be found in dozens of languages – more than 40, at the last count – through some 280 courses. And in addition, the company also offers courses in music, math, and chess – in fact, chess has become the fastest growing course on the site.

The core product, however, remains the language courses. This is where Duolingo excels. Users can find such major tongues as English, Chinese, French, Spanish, and Arabic, as well as courses in Japanese, German, Dutch – it’s a long list. Smaller languages, such as Zulu, Navajo, or Yiddish, are also included, as well as ‘specialty’ or fantasy languages like Klingon.

All of this has powered Duolingo’s growth, which has been substantial. Duolingo launched in 2012, and in the years since it has become the world’s leading mobile language-learning app. As of the end of 3Q25, the company boasted over 50 million daily active users, and more than 135 million monthly active users. These figures were up 36% and 20% year-over-year, respectively. Paid subscribers, at 11.5 million, were up 34% year-over-year.

Duolingo generated $271.7 million in revenue during 3Q25, a total that was up an impressive 41% year-over-year – and that beat the forecast by $11.36 million. At the bottom line, the company realized $5.95 in EPS; we should note that this figure, which exceeded expectations by $5.19 per share, was heavily skewed by a one-time income tax benefit recorded in the quarter.

We should also note that Duolingo’s stock has been falling in recent months. Recent factors affecting the drop include weaker than expected Q4 guidance on bookings, and a strategic shift by the company to favor long-term growth over short-term gains – both factors indicate a potential slow-down in the coming months.

In his write-up on Duolingo, analyst Ramnani focuses on the company’s extensive use of AI technology as a firm support for a quality app, stating, “The company is using GenAI for content generation and has notably launched 140+ new language courses over the past year vs. 100+ over 12 years prior. The company is also already utilizing GenAI coding tools to develop new products, and we believe its deep user data and expertise in gamifying learning/engaging users will continue to provide defensible advantages even as AI natives enter the market… We see continued opportunity for DUOL to grow through its language courses as 2B+ people are looking to learn a 2nd language. We believe DUOL’s expansion into new subjects is a significant growth lever, with potential for new monetization streams over time.”

Following from this, Ramnani puts a Buy rating on DUOL shares, along with a $245 price target that points to a one-year upside potential of 41%. (To watch Ramnani’s track record, click here)

Duolingo stock holds a Moderate Buy consensus rating on Wall Street, based on 17 reviews that include 10 to Buy, 6 to Hold, and 1 to Sell. The stock is priced at $173.87 and its $260.36 average price target implies a gain of 50% by the end of this year. (See DUOL stock forecast)

data.description:

Is AI showing signs of a bubble, or is it still in the early innings? And will the Fed continue cutting rates? Ken Goldman of the JPMorgan’s Americas Equity Research team argues that resilient fundamentals and easing financial conditions support further upside for U.S. equities, translating into a constructive outlook for the S&P 500.

“We are constructive on the S&P 500’s outlook both in terms of price target (7,500 by YE26) and above-trend earnings growth of 13-15% for at least the next two years (2026 EPS of $315 and 2027 EPS of $355 vs. Consensus of $309 and $352, respectively),” Goldman said. “However, should the Fed cut rates further (due to improving inflation dynamics), we see greater upside with the S&P 500 likely surpassing 8,000 in 2026.”

In an upbeat environment like that, there are sure to be winners. The stock analysts at JPM are looking for those potential winning shares, and they are banging the drums when they see them. Here are two, for a closer look. According to the data from TipRanks, both of these stocks get Strong Buy consensus ratings from Wall Street’s analysts. Here are the details.

Oculis Holding (OCS)

Humans are highly visual-centric, giving neuro-ophthalmic disorders a disproportionate impact on quality of life. Vision can degrade in various ways, and it is difficult to stop the damage or restore lost vision.

Swiss-based Oculis is a biopharma company working on the global scene, where it works to improve eye care and save patients’ vision. The company has developed a differentiated pipeline of assets, including innovative novel product candidates for conditions such as diabetic macular edema (DME) and acute optic neuritis (AON). Oculis has an international footprint, and outside of the Swiss headquarters it has operations in the US and Iceland.

A look at the company’s pipeline shows that Oculis has several drug candidate programs undergoing late-stage clinical studies. One is OCS-01, a potential treatment for DME. This condition is a leading cause of preventable blindness in adults, and affects over 37 million people around the world – that number is expected to rise to 53 million by 2040. Current treatments are invasive, involving ocular implants or direct intravitreal injections – these invasive modes directly and negatively impact patient compliance.

OCS-01 is the first topical eye drop developed to address DME. The company’s two DIAMOND Phase 3 trials are now fully enrolled, with more than 800 patients, and topline data is expected during 2Q26. Oculis plans to make its NDA submission to the FDA during 2H26. We should also note that Oculis estimates the DME market at $5 billion globally.

The company is also working on privosegtor (OCS-05), targeting both AON and NAION. Both conditions can cause sudden painless vision loss. The drug candidate, privosegtor, is a non-peptoid small molecule capable of crossing both the blood-brain and retinal barriers, and Oculis has several clinical trials in the works. PIONEER-1 got started during 4Q25, while PIONEER-2 and PIONEER-3 are scheduled for initiation in 1H26 and mid-2026. The company recently reported positive results from the ACUITY Phase 2 trials.

At the end of Q3 this year, Oculis had $182.2 million in cash and liquid assets on hand. A capital raise campaign in October increased that to $300 million in gross cash.

For JPM’s Tessa Romero, the quality and diversification of the pipeline here are key factors. As the analyst writes, “We view Oculis as offering a diversified ophthalmology pipeline with multiple shots on goal in eye indications with high/clear unmet need. In the lead indication of diabetic macular edema (DME) with its most advanced asset OCS-01, Oculis has demonstrated supportive efficacy and safety with its non-invasive and patient-friendly eye drop approach; given the prior data and validated mechanism, we believe OCS-01 has a high likelihood of success. We expect topline results from the phase 3 stage 2 DIAMOND-1 and -2 trials in 2Q26 that, if positive, would enable a potential NDA submission in 2H26, and could represent a near-term source of upside.”

Looking ahead, Romero adds, “Beyond OCS-01, we see its second lead asset, privosegtor (OCS-05), as compelling across a range of neuro-ophthalmology indications for which neuroprotection could provide a meaningful benefit for patients. We view shares as well positioned for upside as Oculis generates additional data supporting the efficacy and safety for privosegtor in patients in its phase 3 studies, with readouts for the asset over the course of time (2027+).”

To quantify this stance, Romero rates OCS as Overweight, with a $38 price target that points toward an 81% upside in the coming year. (To watch Romero’s track record, click here)

data.title: Insiders Put $1 Million on the Line in These 2 Stocks – Why It Could Pay to Follow Their Move
data.description:
For investors looking for meaningful market signals, few indicators are as telling as insider trading activity. Not the illegal kind, but legally disclosed transactions made by corporate officers, board members, and senior executives – the people with the deepest insight into their companies’ operations and prospects.

A couple of points are worth keeping in mind. Insiders sell shares for many reasons, from diversification to tax planning. Purchases, however, tend to be far more deliberate. Executives typically buy only when they believe the stock is undervalued and positioned to move higher.

To ensure transparency, regulators require these transactions to be publicly reported, giving everyday investors access to this information. Insider buying is most telling when the amounts involved are substantial. Seven-figure purchases are rarely made lightly, and when insiders put $1 million or more into their own stock, it often reflects strong conviction in the company’s outlook – and a signal investors may want to pay attention to.

With that in mind, we used TipRanks’ Insiders’ Hot Stocks tool to pinpoint two companies that fit this profile – each seeing recent insider purchases of about $1 million. Both stocks carry Buy ratings from Wall Street analysts and offer double-digit upside potential in the year ahead. Let’s take a closer look.

Nike (NKE)

We’ll start with Nike, one of the best-known names in sports apparel and shoes – and the owner of one of the world’s most recognizable brands, the Nike swoosh. Nike is a household name, and has built itself into a ~$95.5 billion giant of the industry.

In addition to its marketing expertise, the company is also known for producing quality goods that people want to buy. Linking the iconic Air Jordan shoes to basketball star Michael Jordan was a stroke of genius; Jordan himself has a reputation for quality and pursuit of excellence, which lends to the shoes – and Nike over the years has built on that link to develop a large customer base for its various lines of sports apparel: men’s, women’s, and kids’ athletic clothing; winter gear; and specialized sports shoes such as cleats and running or workout shoes.

Nike is a successful firm and one of the world’s great marketing success stories, but it has hit some hard days in recent years. The company has seen its earnings slip in the last two fiscal years, due to a combination of factors including uncertain consumer moods, more uncertainty in tariff and trade policy, deep discounts that cut into margins, competition in the China markets, and a weak direct-to-consumer segment. This has all caused problems for the stock, and shares in NKE are down over 47% in the last 3 years.

The company’s CEO, Elliot Hill, has been reassuring customers and investors alike that Nike has a turnaround plan. And, last month, he spent just over $1 million to buy 16,388 shares in the company. His personal stake in Nike now stands at well over $15 million.

When we turn to the quarterly results, we see that both revenue and earnings beat the forecast in fiscal 2Q26. The top line, of $12.4 billion, was up a modest 0.4% year-over-year and was $190 million better than had been expected; the bottom line of 53 cents per share beat the estimates by 16 cents.

In her coverage of Nike for Bernstein, analyst Aneesha Sherman points out that the turnaround will take time, with patience required to see the desired results. “Nike’s turnaround is progressing… but slowly and with limited visibility on the upcoming FY,” the analyst said. “The main message of December’s Q2 print was that the turnaround is still in ‘middle innings.’ While we’ve seen strong progress in North America (with growth accelerating, new product resonating, and old product almost completely cleared) and Running (with double-digit growth in every region and order books up for CY2026) there is still more work to do in clearing China inventory, turning around Converse, and restoring margins… We like the signs of progress but see this more as a slow grind than a sharp bounce-back.”

These comments back up Sherman’s Outperform (i.e., Buy) rating, while her $85 price target suggests a 30% one-year upside potential. (To watch Sherman’s track record, click here)

Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX)

Vertex, the first stock Bank of America healthcare pick we’ll look at, is a biopharma company with a strong portfolio of approved, commercialized medications, along with an extensive portfolio of new drug candidates at all levels of development. Vertex boasts that it dedicates upwards of 70% of its operating expenses, and some 3/5 of its workforce, to R&D activities.

This is a golden combination for a biopharmaceutical research firm, giving the company a sound base of revenue-generating products and a pipeline of future products with potential to expand that base. Currently, the largest revenue generator among Vertex’s approved products is Trikafta, a treatment for the lung disease cystic fibrosis. Trikafta has been on the market since 2019 for patients ages 12 and up; in 3Q25, the last period reported, it brought in $2.65 billion in revenue for the company. This was approximately 85% of Vertex’s quarterly revenue in the period. The company’s second leading product, Alyftrek, was approved in 2024 for CF patients ages 6 and up. Alyftrek sales generated $247 million for Vertex in Q3 last year.

The company’s total revenue in Q3 came to $3.08 billion, and marked an 11% increase year-over-year. The Q3 revenue beat the forecast by $20 million. The company’s earnings figure in Q3, the non-GAAP EPS of $4.80, was up 42 cents per share year-over-year and was 22 cents per share better than had been expected.

Shares in Vertex are up more than 11% over the past 12 months, lagging the broader markets. We should note that, in the summer of last year, the stock took a hard hit when it removed the drug candidate VX-993 from its pipeline, following the failure of a clinical study to meet endpoint goals. The stock has still not fully recovered from that hit.

Looking ahead for Vertex, one key factor seems to be povetacicept, a drug candidate under development for the treatment of both IgA nephropathy and primary membranous nephropathy in adult patients. This past November, the company announced positive data from the Phase 1/2 RUBY-3 study, showing statistically significant improvements for patients. These results support Vertex’s Biologics License Application process for povetacicept, while the full enrollment has been completed for the Phase 3 RAINIER clinical trial in IgA nephropathy. On the primary membranous nephropathy track, the drug has been granted Fast Track Designation by the FDA, and a Phase 2/3 pivotal trial has been initiated.

Bank of America’s Tazeen Ahmad has focused on povetacicept as the key support for her upbeat view of VRTX shares, writing, “Near-term, we are focused on the phase 3 readout for povetacicept in IgA nephropathy (IgAN), which we see as the key catalyst for the year. We highlight the company announced in May 2025 that the cohort for the 36-week interim analysis had completed enrollment, so we expect the data update could come in 1Q26. VRTX already initiated the rolling submission to file for accelerated approval, which is expected to complete in 1H26. We think povetacicept has best-in-class potential in IgAN based on early data and the differentiated dosing profile. We see potential for upside from positive results from the interim readout of the phase 3 RAINIER trial providing further validation of prior data. IgAN represents an attractive commercial opportunity with ~300K patients in US and EU, and VRTX has further to expand povetacicept into other indications like primary membranous nephropathy (pMN) and generalized myasthenia gravis (gMG).”

data.description:
Over the past few months, the Federal Reserve has shifted back into easing mode, rolling out a series of interest rate cuts that mark a clear change in policy. The central bank delivered 25-basis-point reductions in September, October, and December, bringing the federal funds rate to a target range of 3.50%–3.75%, its lowest level since 2022.
